Author: Anand Raj
Title: AI Pair Programming Isn’t Magic — It’s Process. Here’s How I Made It Work
Text:
AI Pair Programming Isn’t Magic — It’s Process. Here’s How I Made It Work
“I thought AI pair programming would feel like coding with a genius co-founder. Turns out, it’s more like babysitting a robot that’s great with syntax but terrible at reading between the lines.”
The Expectation
When I first started using AI tools for pair programming, I imagined this magical flow:
- I type a vague idea.
- The AI understands.
- It builds exactly what I want.
- I sip coffee while watching it work.
Sounds dreamy, right?
Yeah… no.
The Reality
What actually happened:
- I gave it a high-level prompt.
- It nodded enthusiastically (“You’re absolutely right!”)
- Then confidently built the wrong thing.
- I sighed, deleted everything, and rewrote it manually.
It wasn’t the AI’s fault — it was mine. I had skipped the most important ingredient in AI-assisted coding:
Process
Once I built a simple, repeatable workflow to work with the AI — not just throw things at it — everything changed.
Here’s what worked for me.
Step 1: Break Big Goals Into Small Chunks
Imagine walking into a room and yelling,
“Can someone build me an app that sends marketing emails to all the right people at the right time?”
And then your intern goes: “On it!”
Only to return 15 minutes later with… a random React component and an untested backend function that sends real emails to real people. 😬
What works better:
- Break the task into clear modules → Think: one function, one API, one UI component.
- “Create an API endpoint that takes a list of emails and logs them — no actual sending.”
- “Design a DB schema for storing email templates. Just schema, not the insert/update logic.” - Write out the purpose, inputs, and expected outputs → Don’t assume it knows what you mean.
- Include edge cases if you can.
- Small steps = fewer surprises.
When I started giving it bite-sized tasks, Cursor stopped hallucinating and started being useful.
What I learned is: AI can help — but only if you ask it to do one small, clear thing at a time.
Step 2: Prompt Like You’re Writing Instructions and Giving a brief
Writing good prompts is 90% of the work.
Here’s a prompt I used early on:
“Build an API for sending emails.”
Here’s what I say now:
“I need a POST API called
/send-email
. It should accept recipient email, subject, and body. Use Gmail APIs. Don’t send real emails yet — just log the output.”
Big difference.
The second one is like giving your pair programmer a proper spec. No guesswork.
Crystal clear. Cursor behaves. Everyone’s happy.
Step 3: Tell It to Wait Before Coding
AI tools are like overenthusiastic junior devs. You say, “Let’s plan this,” and they’re already typing.
So I started adding this to my prompts:
“Do NOT implement anything yet. Just confirm what you understood.”
I ask follow-up questions like:
- “What edge cases should I think about?”
- “What assumptions are you making here?”
That alone saved me hours of rework.
Bonus: Ask it to explain back what it understood.
You’ll be shocked how often it misunderstands even simple tasks. 9 out of 10 times, it gets something wrong — or misses something important. But when I catch it early, I save myself hours of back-and-forth.
Step 4: Don’t Let It Make the Big Calls — Use It for Ideas, Not Authority
AI tools are great at brainstorming options:
I used to ask stuff like:
“What’s the best way to handle retries?”
And Cursor would throw in a retry loop with zero context about how I handle failures, what platform I’m on, or what my users expect.
Now I ask:
“Can you suggest 2–3 retry mechanisms and explain the pros and cons for each?”
Now, I never ask: “What’s the best way?”
Instead, I ask: “What are the trade-offs between these approaches?”
Then I pick the one that fits.
Cursor’s not my architect — it’s the tech buddy I bounce ideas off. I stay the architect.
Step 5: When It Starts Looping, Reset the Room
Cursor sometimes gets… stuck in loops, repeating wrong assumptions, or offering nonsense fixes. When that happens, I don’t argue. I just hit reset.
Tips:
- Open a new chat with a clean slate
- Reframe the prompt
- Or switch to another AI like Gemini or ChatGPT for a fresh take
(Yes, I cross-check AI advice — no shame in that)
Then I bring the new insights back to Cursor, and ask it to adjust. Works like a charm.
Step 6: Get It to Document Its Work — or Regret It Later
Here’s the thing — Cursor has no memory. Unless you remind it, it’ll redo something you already fixed… then pat itself on the back.
Now, after every big change, I ask it to generate a .md
summary of:
“Can you create a short summary of what we just did? Document what changed, why we made the change and what files/methods were affected.”
This way, I can go back later and actually understand what happened. This tiny habit saved me from hours of digging through old prompts.
Plus, it makes you look super organized. (Even if you’re not.)
Step 7: Review Like a Human, Not a Robot
The AI wrote code. It runs. That doesn’t mean it’s good. Cursor can write beautiful code — that absolutely doesn’t work. Or worse, kind of works… but quietly breaks your logic in the background.
Just because the AI wrote it, doesn’t mean it’s right.
Now I review everything line by line it generates:
- Does it actually solve the problem?
- Did it miss error handling?
- Is the logic solid or just copy-pasted from StackOverflow?
If I don’t understand a piece of logic, I make Cursor explain it to me like I’m five.
(“What’s this loop doing exactly?” “Why are we catching a generic exception here?”)
And I don’t just scan the code. I read it like I wrote it — because when it breaks in production, guess who’s debugging?
Step 8: Test Early, Test Small
If you’re building something bigger, test each module before moving on.
Ask the AI to:
- Write unit tests
- Stub out fake data
- Simulate edge cases
Don’t wait until the end to “see if it works.” That’s a trap. I’ve been there. It hurts.
Final Thoughts: AI Won’t Save You — But It’ll Speed You Up (If You Let It)
Here’s the truth no one tells you:
AI pair programming works… but only when you do the thinking.
If you expect the AI to:
- Understand vague prompts
- Make smart architectural decisions
- Catch all the edge cases
…you’re going to have a bad time.
But if you:
- Treat it like a fast, slightly clueless coding buddy
- Stay in control of the plan
- Review everything like a responsible adult
Then yes — it’s a total superpower.
TL;DR — The Non-Magical Magical Process
- Don’t give it huge problems — Break things into small, clear chunks
- Write prompts like you’re writing a spec
- Tell the AI to confirm before it builds
- Stay in charge of decisions
- Review the code like it’s your own
- Test often and early
- Reset when stuck
- Ask it to document everything
There’s no magic. No magic wand.
Just process.
But trust me — once you figure out the rhythm, it’s like having a cheat code.
